---
title: "Environments & packages"
module: "M1 — Python for AI"
chapter: 13
difficulty: "beginner"
prereqs:
  - "M1 Ch1 — Variables & types"
  - "M1 Ch12 — A NumPy preview"
learning_objectives:
  - "Explain why projects isolate their packages in a virtual environment"
  - "Read and write a requirements.txt line that pins a package version"
  - "Recognise pip and micropip as the tools that install packages"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 15
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
review_cards:
  - id: why-venv
    q: "Why do projects use a virtual environment (venv)?"
    a: "To isolate each project's packages, so different projects can use different versions without clashing — and so a project is reproducible."
  - id: pin-version
    q: "What does a requirements.txt line like pandas==2.3.0 do?"
    a: "It pins an exact version, so anyone (and any machine) installs the same package and the project keeps working tomorrow."
  - id: pip-vs-micropip
    q: "What installs packages with pip on a normal machine, and what is its in-browser counterpart?"
    a: "pip installs from PyPI into your environment; in the browser (Pyodide), micropip is the equivalent that loads packages into the in-memory runtime."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=beginner >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

You've imported `numpy` and used `csv` without a thought — but where do those
packages come from, and what happens when two projects need *different* versions of
the same one? Project A wants pandas 1.5, project B wants pandas 2.3. Install one
and you break the other.

The fix every real project uses is a **virtual environment**: a private box of
packages, one per project, that can't clash with anything else. This chapter is the
*what and why* of environments and packages — the habits that keep a project
runnable tomorrow and on someone else's machine.

> You'll need from before: that you `import` packages to use them (Ch6, Ch12), and
> that code runs in the browser here via Pyodide (Ch2).

---

## Packages, and where Python looks for them

A **package** is reusable code someone else wrote — `numpy`, `pandas`, `csv`. When
you `import` one, Python searches a list of folders (`sys.path`) for it. The cell
below is a runnable look at that search path and what's available right now, in this
browser runtime:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Where this Python looks for packages, and one that's already here"
import sys
import numpy

print("Python:", sys.version.split()[0])
print("It searches these folders for packages (first few):")
for path in sys.path[:4]:
    print("  -", path or "(the current folder)")

print("Already installed here — numpy", numpy.__version__)
```

In the browser, packages live in Pyodide's in-memory runtime, and the tool that
adds more is **`micropip`** — the browser counterpart of `pip`. On your own
computer, the tool is **`pip`**, and you keep each project's packages separate with
a **virtual environment**.

## Virtual environments, on your own machine

A **virtual environment** (`venv`) is a folder holding one project's own Python and
its own packages. You create one, "activate" it, and any `pip install` from then on
goes into *that* project alone. When you're done, you record the exact versions in a
**`requirements.txt`** so anyone can rebuild the same setup.

> 💡 **On your own computer** (this page runs in the browser, so the commands below
> are for your real machine, not here). These are real commands; the `pip freeze`
> output is from an actual run:
>
> ```bash
> python -m venv .venv             # create the isolated environment
> source .venv/bin/activate        # turn it on  (Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate)
> pip install wrapt==1.16.0        # install into THIS project only
> pip freeze                       # list exact installed versions
> # -> wrapt==1.16.0
> pip freeze > requirements.txt    # save them so the project is reproducible
> ```

Anyone who later runs `pip install -r requirements.txt` in their own fresh venv gets
the very same versions — the project runs the same on every machine.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 🧱 For Java developers

A `venv` plays the role of an isolated dependency set per project — think of
`requirements.txt` as Python's `pom.xml`/`build.gradle` dependency list, and
`pip`/`micropip` as Maven/Gradle fetching from a registry (PyPI). The big
difference: there's no compile step and no central `target/` — packages install
straight into the active environment, which is why isolating that environment
matters.
:::

> ⚠️ Watch out: pinning exact versions (`==2.3.0`) is what makes a project
> reproducible. An unpinned `pip install pandas` grabs whatever is newest that day,
> so the same code can behave differently next month.

---

## Let's build it (worked example)

You won't run `pip` in the browser, but the **`requirements.txt` format** is plain
text you can build and read with the Python you already know. Here we turn a few
packages into pinned requirement lines. Press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Build requirements.txt lines from names and versions"
wanted = [("numpy", "2.5.0"), ("pandas", "2.3.0"), ("scikit-learn", "1.9.0")]

lines = [f"{name}=={version}" for name, version in wanted]
for line in lines:
    print(line)
```

Each line is `name==version` — the same shape `pip freeze` printed in the transcript
above. Reading and writing this format is all you need to keep a project pinned.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. A **package** is reusable code you `import`; Python finds it on `sys.path`, and
   **pip** (or **micropip** in the browser) installs more.
2. A **virtual environment** gives each project its own packages, so versions can't
   clash between projects.
3. A **`requirements.txt`** of `name==version` lines **pins** exact versions, making
   a project reproducible on any machine.
4. Pin versions deliberately — unpinned installs grab whatever is newest and can
   change behaviour later.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢

Complete `pin` so it builds a `requirements.txt` line from a package name and
version. Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_pin
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_pin
def pin(name, version):
    """Return a requirements line that pins name to version, e.g. 'numpy==2.5.0'."""
    return ____  # TODO: build the "name==version" string

run_tests([
    ("pins numpy", pin("numpy", "2.5.0"), "numpy==2.5.0"),
    ("pins pandas", pin("pandas", "2.3.0"), "pandas==2.3.0"),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

You want the text `name`, then `==`, then `version`. An f-string makes this readable:
`f"{name}=={version}"`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def pin(name, version):
    return f"{name}=={version}"
```

The f-string drops `name` and `version` into the `name==version` shape that pip
reads — exactly what a `requirements.txt` line looks like.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡

Write `parse_requirement(line)` — split a `requirements.txt` line like
`"pandas==2.3.0"` into a `(name, version)` tuple. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_parse
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_parse
def parse_requirement(line):
    """Split 'name==version' into a (name, version) tuple."""
    name, version = ____  # TODO: split line on "==" into two parts
    return name, version

run_tests([
    ("splits pandas", parse_requirement("pandas==2.3.0"), ("pandas", "2.3.0")),
    ("splits numpy", parse_requirement("numpy==2.5.0"), ("numpy", "2.5.0")),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

`"pandas==2.3.0".split("==")` returns the list `["pandas", "2.3.0"]`. Unpacking that
into `name, version` gives you the two pieces.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def parse_requirement(line):
    name, version = line.split("==")
    return name, version
```

`line.split("==")` breaks the text at the `==` into a two-item list, which unpacks
straight into `name` and `version`.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). In two or three sentences, explain
to a teammate why your project should ship a pinned `requirements.txt` rather than
asking everyone to `pip install pandas` with no version. Then write the three
requirement lines your project would pin (pick real packages and versions).

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Explains that pinning gives everyone the **same versions**, so the project is
  reproducible and doesn't break when a package updates.
- Mentions that a **virtual environment** keeps those packages from clashing with
  other projects.
- Lists three `name==version` lines.

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
requirements = [
    "numpy==2.5.0",
    "pandas==2.3.0",
    "matplotlib==3.9.0",
]
print("\n".join(requirements))
```

"Pinning exact versions means everyone installs the same packages, so the code runs
the same on my machine, yours, and the server — and an upstream update next month
can't silently change our results. We install them into a per-project virtual
environment so they never clash with another project's versions."
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch13.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You now know how real projects keep their packages isolated and pinned — the habit
that makes code reproducible. The last Python chapter looks at *where* you write
code in the first place: notebooks, an editor, and free cloud GPUs via Colab — and
when to reach for each.
